ul.li: fence(SeqCst) between bottom write and top read (pop)
fence(SeqCst) between top read and bottom read (steal)

g.text: LIFO vs FIFO: Why the Asymmetry?

div.p: Why Padding? Without padding, bottom and top share a cache line. Owner writes bottom, thieves write top → cache bouncing. Padding ensures each atomic is on its own cache line.

g.text: Owner Pop: LIFO (Last In, First Out)
Thief Steal: FIFO (First In, First Out)

div.p: Memory Safety During Growth Thieves might still be reading the old buffer when we swap pointers. Solutions:

ol.li: Hazard pointers: Track which buffers are in use, defer freeing
Epoch-based reclamation: Global epoch counter, free buffers when all threads have advanced past the growth epoch
Never free: Accept memory leak (simple but wasteful for long-running programs)

g.text: len = bottom - top | number of items
Owner has exclusive | access to [top, bottom)
Thieves only modify | top via CAS

div.p: Why These Invariants Matter The queue is "mostly lock-free" because:

ul.li: Owner's push/pop are mostly non-atomic (single-threaded access to bottom)
Only contention point is CAS on top when queue has 1 item
SeqCst fences ensure all threads see consistent state at critical points Performance implication: Uncontended operations are ~5-10 cycles. Contended (last-item race) is ~50-100 cycles.

text.tspan: // 3. Publish new bottom (thieves can now see task)
// 3. Publish new bottom (thieves can now see task)
// 1. Load top first (what we'll try to claim)
// 2. SeqCst fence ensures we see top BEFORE bottom
// Prevents: seeing new bottom with old top
// 3. Load bottom (owner's view of queue size)
// 4. If t < b, queue is non-empty
// The task at [t] was written before bottom was stored
// 1. Load top first (what we'll try to claim)
// 2. SeqCst fence ensures we see top BEFORE bottom
// Prevents: seeing new bottom with old top
// 3. Load bottom (owner's view of queue size)
// 4. If t < b, queue is non-empty
// The task at [t] was written before bottom was stored
// Pop from bottom - most recently pushed
// Pop from bottom - most recently pushed

div.p: Why LIFO? Cache locality! The most recently pushed task's data is hottest in L1/L2 cache. Running it next maximizes cache hits.

text.tspan: // Steal from top - oldest waiting task

g.text: let task = buffer[t]; // May be cache COLD

text.tspan: // Steal from top - oldest waiting task

div.p: Why FIFO? Fairness! The oldest task has been waiting longest. Stealing it minimizes unfairness and prevents starvation of old tasks.

text.tspan: // Both try to CAS top from t to t+1
// Owner restores bottom and returns None
// Both try to CAS top from t to t+1
// Owner restores bottom and returns None

g.text: if t == b: CAS(top, t, t+1)
if t < b: CAS(top, t, t+1)
